nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Received app response


I can give you the repo-grounded answer for the latest branch. I cannot fetch Railway docs in this session because web access is disabled, so I cannot verify current Railway IaC syntax or service configuration details. I’ll keep the Railway part at the level of deployment shape, not claimed doc-verified syntax.


The core truth in your repo is already clear:


rawr.hq.ts is the composition authority. It builds the manifest with composed oRPC router, workflow trigger router, and Inngest bundle


rawr.hq


.
apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts is the current server boot path: it creates the app, registers core RAWR routes, then mounts enabled server plugins[


bootstrap


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts).
apps/server/src/rawr.ts is the current concrete host mount site for route families, with explicit order: /api/inngest, then /api/workflows/*, then /rpc + /api/orpc/*[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).
The runbook also says host apps mount manifest exports and must not compose capability logic directly[


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/process/runbooks/RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION.md).


So the answer is not “different semantics per deployment mode.” The semantics stay the same. What changes is which host entrypoint mounts which subset of the manifest.


The final model should look like this.


You keep one host bundle:


rawr.hq.ts

packages/*

services/* if/when you formalize them

plugins/*

host entrypoints under apps/* or hosts/*


Then you have runtime-role entrypoints:


server

async

cli

optional web


Single process, separate processes, and separate machines are just three ways of deploying those same runtime roles.


What the actual mount sites should look like:


For single-process mode, the simplest truthful shape is one entrypoint that mounts both boundary and async surfaces into one app process.


Right now, the closest existing mount site is apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts calling registerRawrRoutes(...), and then registerRawrRoutes(...) mounts all three route families from the manifest


bootstrap


[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So single-process mode can simply remain a variant of today’s server host.


It would look like this:


rawr.hq.ts
  -> compose:
     - manifest.orpc.router
     - manifest.workflows.triggerRouter
     - manifest.inngest.{client,functions,handler}

apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts
  -> create app
  -> registerRawrRoutes(app, ...)
  -> mount workspace server plugins
  -> listen


And the effective mounted surfaces are:


single process host
  mounts:
    /api/inngest
    /api/workflows/*
    /rpc
    /api/orpc/*
    plus enabled runtime/server plugins


That is basically your current repo shape already.


For separate-process mode, you should stop pretending one app mounts everything. You should split the host entrypoints, not the manifest.


That means:


rawr.hq.ts still composes everything once.


But now you introduce two mount entrypoints:


apps/server/* mounts only boundary/public surfaces

apps/async/* mounts async/runtime surfaces


Concretely:


rawr.hq.ts
  -> still composes full manifest

apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts
  -> mounts:
     /rpc
     /api/orpc/*
     /api/workflows/*
     public/server plugins

apps/async/src/bootstrap.ts
  -> mounts:
     /api/inngest
     worker/internal async adapters
     maybe internal service routers


That is the cleanest scale-out without changing semantics.


The mount-site code should look structurally like this:


/// apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts
const manifest = rawrHqManifest;

app = registerServerBoundaryRoutes(app, {
  orpcRouter: manifest.orpc.router,
  workflowTriggerRouter: manifest.workflows.triggerRouter,
  enrichContext: manifest.orpc.enrichContext,
});


/// apps/async/src/bootstrap.ts
const manifest = rawrHqManifest;

app = registerAsyncRuntimeRoutes(app, {
  inngestHandler: manifest.inngest.handler,
  inngestFunctions: manifest.inngest.functions,
  internalRouters: manifest.internal?.routers ?? [],
  workerRegistrations: manifest.workers ?? [],
});


That is the right split.


Notice what changes:


the manifest stays shared

the hosts mount only what they own


That matches your repo’s existing architecture direction. The docs already insist host mount responsibilities belong in hosts, while composition belongs in the manifest


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


.


For separate-machines mode, the mount sites are the same as separate-process mode.


That is the key point.


Do not invent a third architecture.


Separate processes and separate machines should use the same host entrypoints:


apps/server

apps/async


The difference is only deployment placement.


So:


same code
same mount sites
different deployment placement


That means if Railway runs them as two services on two different machines, nothing architectural changes in the repo except config:


service A starts apps/server

service B starts apps/async


That is why I said earlier that “same machine vs separate machine” is mostly immaterial to the host model. It matters operationally, but not semantically.


The actual host mounting sites I would lock in are:


rawr.hq.ts
Still the one composition authority.


apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts
Owns caller-facing and published routes.


apps/async/src/bootstrap.ts
Owns Inngest ingress and async runtime adapters.


apps/cli
Already separate by nature. Its package already exists as its own app entrypoint with its own rawr bin surface


package


.


So the final shape in the repo should be:


rawr.hq.ts

apps/
  server/
    src/bootstrap.ts
    src/index.ts
    src/routes/*
  async/
    src/bootstrap.ts
    src/index.ts
    src/runtime/*
  cli/
    ...
  web/   optional


Now, the practical mounted surfaces for each mode:


Single process:


apps/server
  mounts:
    /rpc
    /api/orpc/*
    /api/workflows/*
    /api/inngest


Separate processes:


apps/server
  mounts:
    /rpc
    /api/orpc/*
    /api/workflows/*

apps/async
  mounts:
    /api/inngest
    internal async surfaces if needed


Separate machines:


machine/service A -> apps/server
machine/service B -> apps/async

same mounts as above


Now the subtle part: what should live in apps/async beyond /api/inngest?


If you follow the architecture we just worked out, apps/async should be the mount site for:


rawrHqManifest.inngest.handler

worker plugin registrations

optional internal/private service routers for trusted internal callers

schedules/consumers that bridge external events into Inngest or private services


So I would give the manifest one more explicit namespace eventually:


export const rawrHqManifest = {
  orpc: { ... },
  workflows: { ... },
  inngest: { ... },
  workers: {
    routers: [...],
    schedules: [...],
    consumers: [...],
  },
} as const;


Then the hosts become trivial:


server mounts orpc and workflows

async mounts inngest and workers


That is the clean final host model.


Now to the Railway part, carefully.


Because I cannot fetch Railway docs here, I will not claim exact railway.json or service YAML syntax. But the deployment mapping should be:


For single-process mode:


one Railway service runs apps/server

that service exposes one public port


For separate-process mode on Railway:


either one service starts two processes under a process manager, or more simply

two Railway services, one for apps/server and one for apps/async


For separate-machines mode:


in Railway terms this is usually still just separate services/deployments

server public

async private/internal if possible

same repo, same IaC project, different service entry commands


So the Railway deployment model should mirror runtime roles, not plugin kinds.


That means your eventual IaC concept should be:


services:
  server:
    start: bun apps/server/src/index.ts
    public: true

  async:
    start: bun apps/async/src/index.ts
    public: internal-only or restricted


Maybe plus web later.


The sanity check is:


Do not deploy one machine per plugin.
Do not mount async and boundary routes in different files depending on environment.
Do not let deployment mode alter composition semantics.


Instead:


one manifest

stable host entrypoints

deployment just decides which entrypoints run where


That is the clean answer.


If you want the shortest concrete recommendation:


Right now:


keep apps/server as the single-process host

introduce apps/async now, even if initially unused or minimally used


Then:


move /api/inngest mount ownership into apps/async

keep /rpc, /api/orpc/*, /api/workflows/* in apps/server

let Railway run one or both, depending on chosen mode


That gives you a stable path from:


one service
to

two services
without changing the mental model or plugin semantics.

nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 34s


Yes. I checked the Railway docs and re-checked your current repo shape.


The core answer is this:


Your host model should not change across deployment modes.
What changes is only which runtime entrypoints you run, and where.


So the final model is:


one HQ host bundle / one composition authority

a small number of runtime roles

deployment decides whether those roles run:
in one process,

in separate processes,

or in separate Railway services/machines


Railway supports exactly the kind of split you want here: monorepos can deploy as multiple services with different start commands, and services in the same project/environment can talk over private networking using *.railway.internal internal DNS names. Public exposure is separate from private service-to-service communication. Railway Docs+3Railway Docs+3Railway Docs+3


Now the repo-specific part.


From the latest branch snapshot I inspected, your current mounting center is still:


rawr.hq.ts as the composition authority

apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts as the boot path

apps/server/src/rawr.ts as the concrete route mount site


And that current server host mounts:


/api/inngest

/api/workflows/*

/rpc

/api/orpc/*


in explicit order.


So today’s repo already embodies the single-process host form.


What it wants next is not a new semantic model. It wants one more runtime entrypoint.


The final host model I would lock is:


rawr.hq.ts          // composition authority
apps/server/*       // boundary/public runtime role
apps/async/*        // async/durable runtime role
apps/cli/*          // cli runtime role
apps/web/*          // optional web runtime role


That same model supports all three deployment modes.


For single process, the actual host mounting site can remain essentially what you already have in apps/server.


Meaning:


apps/server
  mounts:
    /rpc
    /api/orpc/*
    /api/workflows/*
    /api/inngest
    server/web plugins as applicable


This is the simplest mode.
One Railway service, one start command, one public port. Railway supports overriding service start commands in monorepos, so this can simply be the service whose start command points at your server app. Railway Docs+1


For separate processes, the actual mount sites should split by runtime role, not by plugin type.


That means:


apps/server
  mounts:
    /rpc
    /api/orpc/*
    /api/workflows/*

apps/async
  mounts:
    /api/inngest
    worker runtime registrations
    schedules / cron bridges
    optional internal private service routers


This is the best next step.


It preserves one host bundle and one manifest, but splits:


caller-facing request traffic

durable/background execution


into different processes.


On Railway, this should usually be modeled as two services in the same project/environment, each with its own start command, instead of trying to shoehorn multiple long-running processes into one service. Railway’s monorepo support and per-service start commands fit this cleanly, and same-project services can use private DNS to call each other internally. Railway Docs+2Railway Docs+2


For separate machines, the mount sites should be exactly the same as separate-process mode.


That is the important part.


Do not invent a third architecture.


Use the same code layout:


apps/server
apps/async


and just place them on different Railway services/machines.


So in Railway terms:


Service: server
  start command -> apps/server entrypoint
  public networking enabled

Service: async
  start command -> apps/async entrypoint
  public networking usually disabled unless needed for ingress design
  private networking used for internal calls


Railway private networking is runtime-only, scoped to a project/environment, and gives each service an internal service-name.railway.internal hostname. Internal traffic stays private and does not need public exposure. Railway Docs+2Railway Docs+2


So the concrete recommendation is:


For your repo, the actual mounting sites should become:


rawr.hq.ts
  -> composes full manifest

apps/server/src/bootstrap.ts
  -> mounts public boundary surfaces only

apps/async/src/bootstrap.ts
  -> mounts async runtime surfaces only


Concretely, that means:


Single process


keep using apps/server as today

mount everything there


Separate processes


create apps/async

move /api/inngest ownership there

keep /rpc, /api/orpc/*, /api/workflows/* in apps/server


Separate machines


same as separate processes

just deploy apps/server and apps/async as separate Railway services


The one thing I would not do is deploy individual plugins as individual Railway services.


That is the wrong unit.


Your deployment unit should be runtime role, not plugin kind.


So:


server is a deployable unit

async is a deployable unit

maybe later a domain gets promoted into its own host bundle

but not “one machine per plugin”


That breaks the model and creates ops sprawl fast.


The clean final host model is:


One host bundle
  -> one composition authority
  -> stable runtime-role entrypoints
  -> deploy those entrypoints together or separately


If you want the sharpest version:


Start by adding apps/async now, even if it initially mounts only /api/inngest.
That gives you a stable mount site for:


Inngest runtime ingress

worker plugin registrations

schedules

internal/private async routers


Then Railway deployment becomes trivial:


one service runs apps/server

one service runs apps/async

both live in the same project/environment

internal calls use *.railway.internal if needed Railway Docs+1


That is the host model that supports all three modes without changing semantics.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Okay so overall, what do you think of our architecture in terms of its standalone design? Just from a systems design perspective
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Overall: it is strong. Stronger than most early-stage architecture work because it is actually trying to preserve semantic truth instead of just shuffling folders.


The best part of it is that it already has a real systems spine.


You have a clean separation between:


shared capability logic in packages,

runtime surface adapters in plugins,

composition authority in rawr.hq.ts,

and host mounting in app entrypoints.


That is not cosmetic. That is the core reason the architecture has a chance to scale without turning into soup. The docs and code are aligned on this point: capability logic belongs in packages/*, runtime adapters in plugins/*, final composition in rawr.hq.ts, and hosts mount composed surfaces rather than wiring capabilities directly


SYSTEM


[


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/process/runbooks/RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION.md)[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts).


The second major strength is that you did not collapse unlike things into one fake abstraction. The workflow architecture explicitly preserves the split between caller-facing boundary APIs and durable execution ingress, instead of pretending those are one surface. That is correct. /api/workflows/<capability>/* is caller-facing, /rpc is first-party/internal, and /api/inngest is runtime ingress only


ARCHITECTURE


[


08-workflow-api-boundaries


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/axes/08-workflow-api-boundaries.md). From a systems design perspective, that is one of the most mature choices in the whole design, because it prevents long-term auth, observability, and control-plane confusion.


Third, the architecture is unusually honest about composition authority. A lot of systems say they are modular, but the real composition leaks everywhere. Yours is aiming to keep that centralized in rawr.hq.ts and keep host responsibilities explicit. That is exactly the right place to be strict. rawr.hq.ts already composes the oRPC router, workflow trigger router, and Inngest bundle into one manifest-like export


rawr.hq


. That is a good systems move because it creates a single place where topology becomes concrete.


So the high-level verdict is:
the architecture has a real backbone, a real routing model, and a real path from monolithic deployment to scale-out deployment. That is rare.


Now the harder part: where it is still weak or at risk.


The biggest risk is semantic overexpansion.


You are building a system that wants to be:


local-first,

plugin-extensible,

multi-surface,

typed,

workflow-capable,

multi-host capable,

and potentially domain-federated later.


That is a lot. Each individual idea is reasonable. The failure mode is not that any one piece is wrong. The failure mode is that the combined semantic surface becomes too rich for humans and agents to operate reliably.


Put bluntly: the architecture is closer to “promising framework substrate” than to “stable product platform.” That is fine, but it means you have to keep killing abstraction branches aggressively.


The strongest example is the tension around “host,” “service,” “worker,” “plugin,” and “workflow.” You are close to resolving it, but not fully there. Right now the architecture is good enough that the answer exists, but not yet so crisp that a new contributor or agent would implement it consistently without drift.


My view is that you should lock the following and stop reopening them unless real implementation pain proves them wrong:


A host is a deployable runtime bundle.
A runtime role is a process class inside that host bundle.
A plugin is a surface/runtime adapter, not a deployment unit.
A package is shared capability logic, not a host.
A service boundary is a private callable capability seam, transport-flexible but in-process by default.


Once that is locked, a lot of the remaining fuzz disappears.


The second risk is dual-path creep.


Your docs are already fighting this, correctly. They explicitly resist two equal first-class ways to do the same workflow behavior


ARCHITECTURE


[


08-workflow-api-boundaries


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/axes/08-workflow-api-boundaries.md). Keep that discipline. Because this architecture will get ugly fast if you allow:


direct local async execution,

queue-based workers,

Inngest workflows,

cron-driven handlers,

and ad hoc plugin daemons


all to coexist as equally valid business-logic paths.


That will destroy the elegance.


So from a systems perspective, the architecture is good only if you remain ruthless about canonical paths.


Right now, I think the best canonical path is:


synchronous/public boundary work via API plugins

durable business async via workflow plugins/Inngest

async-host adapters via worker plugins only when needed as bridges or internal runtime seams

internal capability reuse via in-process private service clients by default


That is coherent.
Do not let it become more plural than that unless reality forces it.


The third risk is that the architecture is “hostable” before it is “operable.”


That means the design is already good at saying where code should live, but less proven at:


how config is layered,

how instances differ safely,

how services discover each other,

how internal auth works across split hosts,

how resource controls and concurrency are set,

how failure domains are surfaced and tested.


Some of this is covered directionally in the docs, especially around mount order, context envelopes, and composition guarantees


ARCHITECTURE


[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts). But from a systems design perspective, the thing still needs a clearer operational contract for scale-out mode.


That is not a condemnation. It is exactly what I would expect at this stage. The architecture is ahead of the runtime hardening.


The fourth risk is overfitting to elegance.


There is a real temptation in a system like this to make every concept first-class because the overall model is so intellectually clean. That is where bad frameworks come from.


The architecture stays good only if you continue to prefer:


two or three host roles instead of seven,

one manifest authority instead of composition helpers everywhere,

one default async substrate instead of several,

and one private service model instead of network-first internal RPC everywhere.


This is the sanity check I would apply:


For every new abstraction:
Does it remove a current implementation burden?
Or does it mainly preserve optional future purity?


If it is the second one, kill it.


Now the positive side again, because there is a lot of real value here.


The architecture has very good migration geometry.


That may be its best systems-design property.


It can start as:


one process,

one host entrypoint,

one machine.


Then evolve to:


separate server and async processes,

then separate Railway services,

then perhaps separate host bundles for domains that truly graduate.


That migration ladder is extremely important. It means the architecture is not only clean, but staged. A lot of theoretically clean designs fail because the step from now to later is discontinuous. Yours does not have that problem if you keep the manifest/host-role model.


That is why I think the architecture is genuinely good.


If I had to summarize the system-design verdict in one sentence:


It is a high-quality architecture with real semantic integrity, but it is one abstraction layer away from becoming overbuilt unless you freeze the core nouns and enforce a tiny number of canonical execution paths.


My direct rating, as a standalone systems design:


Conceptual architecture: 8.5/10
Current implementation maturity relative to ambition: 6.5/10
Chance of scaling cleanly if you stay disciplined: high
Chance of collapsing into elegant complexity if you indulge every abstraction urge: also high


The core is good. Very good.
The danger is not bad design.
The danger is designing too much because the design language is already powerful enough to tempt that.


The move now is not to rethink the architecture. The move is to harden and narrow it:


lock the host-role model,

add apps/async,

formalize private service client transport modes,

define worker plugins narrowly,

and stop creating new top-level semantics unless implementation forces them.


That is the path where this becomes a real systems architecture instead of a beautiful meta-architecture.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Okay. Let’s say we draw the boundaries you said we need to draw and pull the whole thing off correctly.


How well suited and novel is this architecture for the specific goal it is trying to achieve?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
If you actually draw the boundaries correctly and keep the execution paths disciplined, this architecture is very well suited to the goal.


And the goal matters here, because this is not “just another backend architecture.”


What you are actually trying to achieve is something like:


a local-first or infra-portable HQ/workbench system, with one semantic core, multiple runtime surfaces, strong typed contracts, pluggable capability expansion, durable async execution, and a path from single-instance simplicity to multi-host growth.


For that goal, the architecture is unusually well matched.


Why it is well suited:


The architecture is built around semantic separation instead of framework separation. That is exactly right for a system whose real problem is not serving HTTP, but organizing capability logic across many surfaces. Your docs and code already align on this: packages hold shared capability logic, plugins adapt it to runtime surfaces, rawr.hq.ts composes the full runtime view, and host apps mount the composed result rather than wiring capabilities ad hoc


SYSTEM


[


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/process/runbooks/RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION.md)[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts). That gives you a real chance at preserving one mental model across API, workflow, CLI, and future host roles.


It is also well suited because it treats durable execution as a first-class runtime concern without polluting every other surface with workflow semantics. That is one of the best design choices in the whole system. The workflow docs explicitly preserve the split between caller-facing trigger APIs and runtime-only Inngest ingress, rather than collapsing them into one fake universal endpoint


ARCHITECTURE


[


08-workflow-api-boundaries


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/axes/08-workflow-api-boundaries.md). For your goal, that matters a lot: you want orchestration, but you do not want everything to become orchestration-shaped.


It is also well suited because it has a real migration ladder. It can honestly start with one host process, then split into server and async roles, then later split those across Railway services or even into separate host bundles, without forcing a rewrite of the core semantics. That is implied by the current manifest/host split and by the explicit mount responsibilities in the repo


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts). For the goal you have, that is crucial, because the architecture needs to support both “I just need to run this thing” and “this domain is now its own ecosystem.”


So from a fitness-to-purpose standpoint, the answer is:
very good, as long as the boundaries stay sharp.


Now the novelty question.


It is novel, but not in the sense of inventing brand-new primitives.


The primitives themselves are not novel:


typed contracts

plugins

workflow engines

host composition

internal vs external boundaries

monorepo package layering


None of that is individually new.


What is more novel is the combination and the center of gravity.


Most systems choose one of these as the organizing principle:


deployable services

framework modules

HTTP routes

package boundaries

workflow graph

plugin marketplace


Your architecture is doing something more specific:
it uses capability composition as the primary organizing principle, then projects that capability into multiple runtime surfaces through controlled adapters, all under one host bundle and one composition authority.


That combination is less common.


The thing it most resembles is a hybrid of:


a typed internal platform

a plugin-oriented local-first application substrate

a workflow-capable backend kernel

and a future host-federation model


That is not a standard off-the-shelf architecture pattern. It is closer to a custom systems substrate.


So I would say:


It is moderately novel architecturally, but highly distinctive in composition and intended use.


The novelty is not “nobody has ever done any of this.”
The novelty is “few people combine these concerns into one coherent bounded architecture with this exact locus of control.”


That matters because novelty cuts both ways.


Good novelty:


one semantic core across many surfaces

one composition authority

host-role scale-out without semantic drift

plugin model that is more serious than frontend widget injection

durable execution built in without making everything workflow-native


Bad novelty risk:


the architecture can become too self-authored

fewer external reference systems match it exactly

contributors may not know the right defaults intuitively

tooling and docs discipline have to do more work than in a conventional service-per-domain setup


So the question is not just “is it novel?”
It is “is the novelty buying something real?”


In your case, yes — if the product goal remains what it appears to be.


This architecture is especially well suited if the goal is:


one HQ/runtime that can be extended by capability

shared logic reused across API, workflow, CLI, and perhaps web/agent surfaces

local-first development with infra-portable deployment

typed and enforceable boundaries

gradual promotion of capabilities into more independent runtime roles or even their own hosts


It is less well suited if the real eventual product turns out to be something simpler, like:


just a conventional SaaS backend with a few jobs

a standard microservice estate

or a pure workflow application


In those cases, this architecture would be overqualified and too concept-heavy.


That is the main sanity check.


Here is the blunt version:


If the goal is genuinely “build a reusable HQ substrate for capability-centric systems that can live locally, scale operationally, and unify many execution surfaces,” then this architecture is one of the better shapes you could choose.


If the goal quietly collapses into “ship a normal app,” then this architecture is too fancy.


Assumptions and uncertainty ledger:


The assessment above assumes the true goal is the broader host/plugin/capability platform you’ve been steering toward, not merely the current template repo as a simple app shell. That assumption is supported by the current docs’ emphasis on multi-surface composition, host mounting, workflow separation, and plugin roots


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The main uncertainty is not whether the architecture can serve that goal, but whether the product and implementation discipline will continue to justify that level of architectural ambition.


My clean verdict:


It is well suited.
It is somewhat novel in architecture, more novel in synthesis.
Its strongest advantage is not cleverness, but that it aligns structure with the real problem.
Its biggest danger is that the novelty tempts you to keep inventing instead of freezing the model and exploiting it.

nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 40s


There is overlap, but not “same thing with different branding.”


The closest answer is:


Your architecture overlaps with OpenClaw/NanoClaw at the plugin-and-hostability layer, but diverges hard at the center of gravity.


OpenClaw and NanoClaw are primarily agent runtimes / personal-assistant systems. Your RAWR architecture is trying to be a capability-composition substrate that can project one semantic core into API, workflow, CLI, web, and host roles.


That sounds subtle, but it is the whole difference.


OpenClaw’s center is a Gateway control plane with channels, tools, skills, and plugins running around it. Its repo/docs describe a single WebSocket Gateway control plane, optional apps/nodes around it, workspace skills under ~/.openclaw/workspace/skills/<skill>/SKILL.md, and a plugin system that can register Gateway RPC methods, HTTP routes, tools, CLI commands, background services, and context engines. Those plugins are loaded in-process with the Gateway and share its trust boundary. OpenClaw+5GitHub+5GitHub+5


NanoClaw’s center is even more specific: it is a single-process Claude-agent orchestrator with per-group container isolation, channels like WhatsApp/Telegram/Slack/Gmail, scheduled tasks, and a strong “don’t add features, add skills” philosophy. Its README explicitly says “one process,” “no microservices,” agents run in isolated containers, channels are added via skills, and the architecture is Channels --> SQLite --> Polling loop --> Container (Claude Agent SDK) --> Response. GitHub+2GitHub+2


RAWR, by contrast, is already documented and coded around a different composition model: shared capability logic in packages/*, runtime adapters in plugins/*, final cross-surface composition in rawr.hq.ts, and host apps mounting those exports rather than owning capability wiring. Its workflow model also explicitly separates caller-facing workflow routes from runtime-only Inngest ingress.


SYSTEM


[


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/process/runbooks/RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION.md)[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts)[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md)


So the overlap is real, but it is partial.


Where the overlap is strongest:


RAWR and OpenClaw both care about extensibility through plugins/skills, multiple runtime surfaces, and not hardcoding every capability into core. OpenClaw plugins can add tools, HTTP routes, RPC methods, CLI commands, and background services, while your RAWR plugin model similarly splits runtime adapters by surface root like plugins/api/*, plugins/workflows/*, plugins/cli/*, plugins/web/*, and more. OpenClaw


PLUGINS


RAWR and NanoClaw both care about keeping the hostable unit understandable and deployable in more than one mode. NanoClaw pushes that to the extreme with one process plus containers; your system pushes it toward one composition authority plus multiple host roles. That is philosophically adjacent, but not identical. GitHub+1


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


Where the overlap is weak:


OpenClaw/NanoClaw are organized around chat-first agent operation. Channels, group/session memory, agent loops, and skills are first-class. RAWR is organized around capability-first composition and only then projects capabilities into surfaces like API/workflow/CLI. That is a different root abstraction. GitHub+3GitHub+3GitHub+3


SYSTEM


OpenClaw’s plugin system is intentionally in-process with the Gateway. Its docs are blunt that plugins are not sandboxed and share the same process-level trust boundary as core. That is much closer to a big extensible runtime than to your host-role / manifest-composition model. OpenClaw


NanoClaw is intentionally one-process and anti-microservice. Your architecture is explicitly trying to support one process now and later split into server/async host roles without changing semantics. Those are different goals. GitHub+1


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


So: would it be “the same” to just use OpenClaw or NanoClaw and rely on their plugins?


No.


It would only be the same if your real goal were:
“build or customize a personal/organizational chat-centric agent runtime with channels, skills, and tools.”


If that were the goal, especially for a sovereign personal assistant or internal ops bot, NanoClaw or OpenClaw could cover a lot. NanoClaw in particular is very attractive if you want a small, container-isolated Claude-centric orchestrator with channels and scheduled jobs. GitHub+1


But for the thing RAWR is aiming at, they are not substitutes. They are closer to possible runtime targets or adjacent inspiration than to drop-in replacements.


Here is the key break:


Your architecture wants one semantic capability core to back:


internal/private service boundaries

public APIs

workflow triggers and durable execution

CLI surfaces

optional web/agent surfaces

multiple host roles and deployment modes


That is much broader and more systemically structured than “agent runtime + plugins.” Your docs already make this explicit in the separation of packages, plugins, manifest composition, and host mounting, plus the split between /rpc, /api/workflows/*, and /api/inngest.


SYSTEM


[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts)[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts)[


08-workflow-api-boundaries


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/axes/08-workflow-api-boundaries.md)


OpenClaw’s plugin surface is powerful, but it is still subordinate to the Gateway runtime. NanoClaw’s skills philosophy is elegant, but it is still subordinate to a single-process Claude-agent orchestrator. GitHub+3OpenClaw+3OpenClaw+3


That means if you tried to “just use them,” one of two things would happen:


First possibility: you bend your product to their architecture. Then you get faster progress at the cost of losing your actual semantic model.


Second possibility: you keep your semantic model and keep extending their runtime until it starts to look like your own system anyway. Then you inherit their assumptions and still end up building your own substrate on top.


That second path is the trap. It feels faster at first and then becomes worse than building your own cleanly.


My judgment:


Use OpenClaw/NanoClaw if your primary goal is an agentic operating environment.


Do not use them as the foundation for RAWR’s core host/capability architecture unless you are willing to make RAWR subordinate to an agent runtime.


That is the real trade.


The most useful way to think about them is this:


OpenClaw gives you a rich, Gateway-centered extensible assistant platform. GitHub+1
NanoClaw gives you a minimal, container-isolated, one-process assistant runtime with channels and skills. GitHub+2GitHub+2
RAWR is trying to be a capability/host substrate that could, if desired, host an agent runtime as one surface among others.


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md)


So the overlap is probably:


OpenClaw: maybe 35–45%

NanoClaw: maybe 20–30%


High overlap in “extensible hostable AI system” vibes.
Much lower overlap in actual architectural purpose.


Sanity check against your goal:


If the sentence is “I want one portable assistant system with skills/plugins/channels,” OpenClaw/NanoClaw are directly relevant.
If the sentence is “I want one typed capability substrate that can expose APIs, workflows, CLI, and multiple host roles and maybe agent surfaces too,” then no, they are not the same thing.


The simpler path is not automatically the better path here. It is only better if it preserves the real center of gravity of what you are building.

nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 12s


Thought for 39s


After looking back at the repo docs and the direction in this project, I do not think RAWR is fundamentally “an assistant runtime with plugins.”


I think RAWR is meant to be a hostable capability substrate for an AI-native headquarters or workbench.


More concretely, RAWR looks like it is trying to be:


a local-first, infra-portable, composition-first operating layer where one semantic capability core can be projected into multiple runtime surfaces — CLI, API, workflow, web, agents, optional MCP — with one composition authority and clear host-mount seams. That is exactly how the repo frames it: shared capability logic in packages/*, runtime adapters in plugins/*, final multi-surface assembly in rawr.hq.ts, and host mounting in apps/*. It also explicitly exports ORPC (OpenAPI and RPC) surfaces, Inngest bundles, web mounts, CLI commands, agent capabilities, and optional MCP actions from that single composition layer.


SYSTEM


That means the core product philosophy of RAWR is not “give the user a bot.”
It is closer to “give the user a portable HQ kernel that can host many surfaces and execution modes around shared capabilities.”


That is why the overlap with OpenClaw and NanoClaw is real but still not decisive.


OpenClaw’s philosophy is centered on a Gateway that keeps an assistant running continuously: it listens on channels, routes messages to the agent, handles scheduled tasks, and manages tool execution. Its plugin model is broad, but still fundamentally subordinate to that gateway runtime. Plugins can add RPC methods, HTTP handlers, tools, CLI commands, background services, and skills, and they run in-process with the Gateway as trusted code. OpenClaw Guide+1


NanoClaw’s philosophy is even more explicit: it is a lightweight personal Claude assistant, intentionally one-process, channel-centric, container-isolated, and minimal. Its own positioning stresses “one process,” “no microservices,” and a personal assistant model with channels, SQLite memory, scheduled tasks, and containerized agent execution. NanoClaws.io+2Nanoclaw AI+2


RAWR is different at the level that matters most: its center of gravity is not chat, channel routing, or even agent orchestration. Its center is capability composition and host composition. The docs call out plugin roots not just for agents, but for CLI, web, API, workflows, and optional MCP, and they make rawr.hq.ts the single composition authority for all of them.


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md)[


RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/process/runbooks/RAWR_HQ_MANIFEST_COMPOSITION.md)


That changes the earlier answer.


No, it would not be the same to “just use OpenClaw or NanoClaw and rely on their plugins.”


That would only be equivalent if RAWR’s real goal were:


“build a persistent agent runtime with channels, tools, scheduled tasks, and plugin-based extensions.”


If that were the goal, then yes, OpenClaw and NanoClaw would be highly relevant foundations. OpenClaw is especially strong if you want a persistent extensible gateway; NanoClaw is strong if you want a compact personal assistant runtime with secure container isolation. Nanoclaw AI+3OpenClaw Guide+3OpenClaw+3


But if RAWR’s actual goal is what the repo and your project direction suggest — a general HQ/workbench substrate where capabilities can be expressed once and mounted into many surfaces and host roles — then OpenClaw and NanoClaw are adjacent systems, not substitutes.


The easiest way to see it is by comparing philosophies, not features.


RAWR’s philosophy appears to be:


one semantic capability core

many runtime surfaces

one composition authority

explicit host seams

infra portability

local-first by default

gradual scale-out from single-process to multiple host roles

agents as one surface among others, not the center of the universe


OpenClaw’s philosophy is:


one always-on gateway assistant

channels and tools are primary

plugins extend the gateway

skills extend assistant behavior

everything trustworthy runs in-process with the gateway OpenClaw Guide+1


NanoClaw’s philosophy is:


one-process personal assistant

Claude-first

container isolation for safety

minimal architecture and low cognitive load

channels, scheduled tasks, and memory as assistant primitives NanoClaws.io+2Nanoclaw AI+2


Those are not the same product philosophies.


RAWR is trying to be more like a substrate.
OpenClaw and NanoClaw are trying to be assistant runtimes.


That means the overlap is best described like this:


There is meaningful overlap in the outer shell:


pluginability

long-running runtime

optional agent surfaces

extensibility

local/self-hosting instincts


But there is much less overlap in the inner thesis:


RAWR wants to organize capabilities and project them across surfaces

OpenClaw/NanoClaw want to organize assistant behavior and channel/tool execution


That is why “just use them” is not a clean substitution.


You would run into one of two bad outcomes.


First bad outcome: you bend RAWR downward into “assistant runtime plus plugins.”
Then you lose the actual breadth of what RAWR is trying to be.


Second bad outcome: you keep RAWR’s broader philosophy and start layering your capability/host/composition model on top of OpenClaw or NanoClaw.
Then their gateway/assistant assumptions become the wrong center, and you end up fighting the base system.


That is the real systems-design answer.


So my revised judgment is:


OpenClaw and NanoClaw are not the same thing as RAWR.
They are closer to:


possible inspiration,

possible optional agent runtimes,

or possible hosted subsystems inside RAWR’s broader world.


But they are not the right replacement for RAWR’s core architecture if RAWR is truly meant to be the broader HQ substrate your docs suggest.


The blunt version:


If RAWR were only an assistant, use them.
If RAWR is an HQ kernel, do not subordinate it to them.


That is the real split.

nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 25s


Yes — with the product docs, it now makes sense to add an agent host inside the RAWR host bundle.


And the reason is simple: RAWR’s own vision documents already place the agent runtime underneath RAWR as a composable substrate rather than as the product itself. The product synthesis doc explicitly says RAWR is the Nx plugin/product, while the “agent dispatch runtime” is Nanoclaw, and RAWR owns the opinions about how Nx, oRPC (OpenAPI and Remote Procedure Call), Inngest, oclif, and Nanoclaw compose. It also says stewards are persistent agent identities with domain scope and accumulated decision history, and V3 is explicitly “the steward runtime: Nanoclaw integration.”


rawr-product-synthesis


So the answer changes from “probably not” to:


Yes, an agent host is consistent with RAWR’s actual product thesis.
But it should be added as one runtime role inside the RAWR host bundle, not as the thing RAWR becomes subordinate to.


That distinction is the whole game.


RAWR, per your product docs, is not trying to be “a better chatbot daemon.” It is trying to make a monorepo structurally agent-navigable and agent-governable through contracts, lanes, gates, dependency topology, scaffolding, migrations, and a steward workbench that turns judgment-heavy tasks into operations like orient.enter, assess.impact, contribute.scaffold, govern.evaluate, and observe.health.


rawr-product-synthesis


steward-workbench-design


That means the core philosophy of RAWR is:


agents are not just “hands”

agents are stewards

stewards need persistent identity, memory, domain scope, and autonomy

but their environment must be structurally constrained and queryable

and the source of truth for that environment is the repo’s contracts, graph, gates, AGENTS metadata, and workbench operations — not freeform assistant runtime behavior


That is very different from OpenClaw and NanoClaw even though they now overlap much more than before.


OpenClaw’s philosophy is Gateway-first. Its docs describe a central Gateway that manages channels, routes messages to agents, handles tools/skills, sessions, and memory, with workspace and shared skills layered around that runtime. Plugins extend that Gateway, and plugins run in-process with it as trusted code. OpenClaw+2OpenClaw+2


NanoClaw’s philosophy is even sharper: one Node.js process, channel-centric, Claude-first, container-isolated agent execution, SQLite persistence for sessions/tasks/router state, and scheduled tasks. It is deliberately a personal or team assistant runtime with per-group isolation, not a structural monorepo operating layer. NanoClaws.io+1


RAWR’s philosophy, with the docs you uploaded, is:


not just persistent agents

but persistent agents operating inside a typed, graph-aware, migration-driven, contract-first environment

where the steward workbench is the operational API for autonomous contribution

and the long-term distinction is explicitly “agent-as-steward” rather than “agent-as-hands”
rawr-product-synthesis


So, to re-answer the earlier question at the level of philosophy, goals, and capabilities:


No, it still would not be the same to “just use OpenClaw/NanoClaw and rely on their plugins.”


Why not?


Because OpenClaw and NanoClaw are primarily agent runtimes.


RAWR is trying to be a structural substrate for stewarded environments.


That means OpenClaw/NanoClaw solve:


persistent identity

channels

sessions

memory

scheduled tasks

tool/skill execution

container or runtime orchestration


RAWR is trying to solve:


how an agent understands where it is

what a change affects

whether a move is structurally legal

how to scaffold the right thing correctly

how to verify it mechanically

when to act versus propose

how to accumulate context gravity over time through contracts, gates, graph, and migrations
rawr-product-synthesis
steward-workbench-design


Those are not interchangeable layers.


So the strongest architecture is:


RAWR host bundle


server host

async host

CLI host

optional web host

agent host


And inside the agent host, you can run:


a single NanoClaw process, or

later an OpenClaw-style Gateway if you want a different runtime,

with RAWR-specific adapter layers around it


That is coherent.


The agent host would then be the runtime role responsible for:


steward identity lifecycle

channel ingress

long-lived conversations

memory/session persistence

agent execution runtime

agent-local tool/skill availability

calling back into RAWR workbench/services/APIs


That fits the product docs very well.


The important part is what does not move into NanoClaw/OpenClaw:


contract authority

boundary authority

repo topology authority

gate authority

workbench authority

scaffolding/migration authority

governance thresholding

capability composition authority


Those remain RAWR’s job.


So yes: add an agent host.
No: do not let the agent runtime become the center of the system.


That also answers the “agent plugins” question.


Yes, agent plugins make sense inside RAWR now.
But they should mean something precise.


They should not just be arbitrary skill folders.


A RAWR plugins/agents/* surface should be the adapter layer that:


exposes selected RAWR capabilities/tools to the agent runtime

registers steward/domain-specific prompts/instructions

binds workbench operations into agent-usable tools

defines channel bindings and steward routing

optionally ships workspace-local skills/memory/config for that steward/domain


That is very different from “whatever the underlying runtime supports.”


So the right model is:


RAWR
  owns:
    structure, contracts, graph, gates, workbench, composition, host roles

Agent runtime (NanoClaw/OpenClaw)
  owns:
    long-running agent execution, channels, sessions, memory, scheduling

RAWR agent plugin
  adapts:
    RAWR capabilities/workbench/identity/routing
    into the runtime’s skill/tool/workspace model


That is the clean boundary.


Now, on agent workspaces and OpenClaw/NanoClaw-style workspace semantics:


Yes, those ideas are useful.
But they should be treated as runtime-local operational context, not as the primary source of system truth.


OpenClaw’s docs explicitly support per-agent workspace skills and shared skills, with workspace precedence over shared skills. OpenClaw
That is good for:


local steward notes

steward-specific skill overlays

temporary domain tactics

runtime memory artifacts

tool wrappers specific to one steward


It is bad as the place where structural truth lives.


For RAWR, the source of truth should still be:


repo contracts

AGENTS metadata

graph

phase gates

workbench APIs

generated structure


So the right split is:


Good to keep in agent workspace


steward-local memory caches

channel/session state

local skill overlays

runtime scratchpads

temporary task context

optional domain-specific operating instructions


Do not keep as primary truth in agent workspace


package boundaries

contract definitions

gate rules

capability registration

migration truth

governance truth

system topology


That keeps the runtime powerful without letting it drift away from the repo substrate.


So the overall recommendation is:


Yes, add an agent host in the RAWR host bundle.
Use NanoClaw as the initial runtime if that still fits your taste and constraints, because your own product doc already positions Nanoclaw as the agent dispatch runtime and V3 as Nanoclaw integration.


rawr-product-synthesis


But add it with these rules:


The agent host is a runtime role, not the product center.


Agent plugins are RAWR adapters into the agent runtime, not just raw runtime plugins.


Agent workspaces are runtime-local overlays, not the canonical source of structure.


The steward workbench remains the main operational API the agents should use to understand and modify the environment. The workbench doc is basically screaming this already: the point is to replace grep/inference/manual coordination with composed operations that answer the actual questions an autonomous steward asks.


steward-workbench-design


So the more complete answer to your original “same as just using OpenClaw/NanoClaw?” question is now:


They are not replacements.
They are excellent runtime substrates for one host role inside RAWR.


That is a big difference.


If you want the blunt conclusion:


RAWR should probably host NanoClaw, not become NanoClaw.
OpenClaw/NanoClaw solve steward runtime. RAWR solves steward environment.


That is the clean product stack.

nodes.say:
Yes, agent plugins make sense inside RAWR now. But they should mean something precise. They should not just be arbitrary skill folders. A RAWR plugins/agents/* surface should be the adapter layer that: • exposes selected RAWR capabilities/tools to the agent runtime • registers steward/domain-specific prompts/instructions • binds workbench operations into agent-usable tools • defines channel bindings and steward routing • optionally ships workspace-local skills/memory/config for that steward/domain


what would this look like concretely?


like how would we adapt our RAWR system to work with the nanoclaw runtime in a pluggable way? Do we just basically allow importing various internal routers? what would one agent “plugin” even represent?


We originally kept agents plugin to mean deployable claude plugins (which also deploy to codex) and get used by the CLI agents themselves directly


Would we need a different concept for the nanoclaw plugins?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. You need a different concept.


Right now your plugins/agents/* idea is overloaded.


You have at least three different things hiding under “agent plugin”:


Authoring/runtime-facing agent capability packs
These are the deployable Claude/Codex-facing skills, prompts, tools, and conventions that CLI agents use directly.

Steward identity/domain packs
These define who an agent is in the RAWR world: domain scope, operating posture, memory shape, allowed workbench operations, routing hints, ingress bindings.

Agent-runtime adapters
These adapt RAWR into a concrete runtime like NanoClaw or OpenClaw.


Those are not the same unit. If you keep calling all three “agent plugins,” the system will drift.


So the clean answer is:


Do not reuse the same concept for both CLI-agent plugins and NanoClaw runtime integration.


You want at least a two-layer model, probably three.


The cleanest structure is:


plugins/agents/*          # agent capability packs for RAWR-native/CLI use
plugins/stewards/*        # steward identity + domain binding packs
plugins/agent-hosts/*     # runtime adapters for NanoClaw/OpenClaw/etc.


If you hate the extra root, then keep plugins/agents/* for the deployable Claude/Codex-facing packs and add a distinct runtime root like:


plugins/agent-runtimes/*


That is probably the best naming compromise.


Because the thing that plugs into NanoClaw is not “an agent” in the same sense as your current agent plugins. It is a host adapter.


Now, concretely, what would the integration look like?


The product docs already give the right split.


RAWR’s role is to provide the structural substrate and steward workbench. The product synthesis says RAWR owns the opinions about how Nx, oRPC, Inngest, oclif, and Nanoclaw compose, and that stewards are persistent agent identities with scope and decision history. The steward workbench doc says the point is to replace manual grep/inference with operational APIs like orient.enter, assess.impact, contribute.scaffold, and govern.evaluate.


rawr-product-synthesis


steward-workbench-design


So the NanoClaw integration should not be “just import internal routers and hope.” That is too raw and too leaky.


You want a formal adapter seam.


The concrete shape should be:


RAWR core
  packages/*
  services/*
  workbench/*
  rawr.hq.ts

RAWR agent runtime adapter
  plugins/agent-runtimes/nanoclaw/*

RAWR steward/domain packs
  plugins/stewards/<steward-or-domain>/*

RAWR CLI/native agent packs
  plugins/agents/<capability-or-pack>/*


And the adapter should do four jobs.


First, it boots the NanoClaw runtime inside the RAWR host bundle’s agent host.


Second, it translates RAWR steward packs into NanoClaw concepts:


identities

channels

tools

memory/session configuration

scheduling

routing


Third, it exposes selected RAWR workbench and service operations as NanoClaw-usable tools.


Fourth, it keeps RAWR as the source of truth for structural knowledge, while NanoClaw remains the long-running runtime for sessions/channels/memory.


That means one NanoClaw adapter plugin probably represents:


“How RAWR hosts and feeds NanoClaw.”


Not:
“One deployable agent.”


That is the key distinction.


So, concretely, one agent-runtime plugin for NanoClaw would likely export something like:


type AgentRuntimePlugin = {
  runtime: "nanoclaw";
  createHost(config: AgentHostConfig): Promise<AgentHostInstance>;
  registerSteward(steward: StewardRegistration): Promise<void>;
  registerToolset(toolset: AgentToolsetRegistration): Promise<void>;
  registerChannels(channels: ChannelBindingRegistration[]): Promise<void>;
  registerMemoryProviders(memory: MemoryProviderRegistration[]): Promise<void>;
};


Then a steward plugin would represent:


type StewardPlugin = {
  stewardId: string;
  domain: string;
  profile: {
    displayName: string;
    mission: string;
    allowedOperations: string[];
    defaultChannels?: string[];
  };
  instructions: string[];
  memoryPolicy?: MemoryPolicy;
  toolsets: string[];
  routing?: StewardRoutingPolicy;
};


And a CLI/Codex/Claude “agent plugin” pack would represent:


type AgentCapabilityPack = {
  packId: string;
  skills: SkillDefinition[];
  prompts: PromptDefinition[];
  commands?: CommandDefinition[];
  localTools?: LocalToolDefinition[];
};


These three get composed, but they are not the same thing.


Now to your question: “Do we just basically allow importing various internal routers?”


Not directly as the primary integration surface.


Internal routers are too low-level to be the ergonomic contract for an agent runtime. They are an implementation substrate, not the runtime abstraction.


The better path is:


define RAWR workbench operations and private service clients as the canonical internal capability seam

then expose selected ones to NanoClaw as tools through the runtime adapter


That means the NanoClaw adapter should depend on curated tool registries, not on arbitrary router imports.


So instead of this:


import { everyInternalRouterInTheWorld } from "...";


you want this:


registerNanoclawToolset({
  steward: "platform-architect",
  tools: [
    workbench.orient.enter,
    workbench.assess.impact,
    workbench.contribute.scaffold,
    workbench.observe.health,
    services.repo.readFile,
    services.graph.findDependents,
  ],
});


That is much better.


Why?


Because agent runtimes want stable tool contracts, not raw router topology.


Now, what should one agent plugin represent?


Given your original meaning, I would preserve it:


plugins/agents/* should continue to mean:
deployable agent capability packs used directly by CLI agents / Claude / Codex.


That preserves continuity and avoids destroying the existing concept.


Then introduce a different concept for NanoClaw-facing runtime integration.


My recommendation:


keep plugins/agents/* = agent capability packs

add plugins/stewards/* = steward identity/domain packs

add plugins/agent-runtimes/* = runtime adapters like NanoClaw


That is the clean version.


If you want fewer roots, the minimum viable separation is:


plugins/agents/*           # existing Claude/Codex/CLI packs
plugins/agent-runtimes/*   # NanoClaw/OpenClaw adapters


and steward identities can live either:


under plugins/agents/* as a subtype, or

under a dedicated stewards/ root outside plugins


I think plugins/stewards/* is semantically stronger, because “steward” is a real first-class noun in your product docs, not just an implementation detail. The docs explicitly distinguish stewards as persistent identities with scope and decision history, and the workbench is explicitly for those steward agents.


rawr-product-synthesis


steward-workbench-design


So the concrete full composition would look like this:


rawr.hq.ts
  composes:
    services/*
    api/workflow/cli/web plugins
    agent capability packs
    steward packs
    agent runtime adapter

apps/agent-host/*
  boots:
    NanoClaw runtime via agent-runtime plugin
  registers:
    steward identities from plugins/stewards/*
    toolsets from RAWR workbench/services
    optional capability packs from plugins/agents/*


Then the NanoClaw adapter maps them into NanoClaw concepts:


steward pack -> agent identity / profile / channel bindings

RAWR workbench/service tools -> NanoClaw tools

capability pack -> optional skill/tool overlays for that runtime

memory policy -> NanoClaw session/store config

schedules -> NanoClaw scheduled task registration


That is the actual pluggable design.


The most important guardrail is this:


Do not let NanoClaw plugins become the canonical place where RAWR semantics live.


RAWR semantics should still live in:


workbench operations

private service boundaries

contracts

graph/gates/metadata

steward pack definitions


The NanoClaw adapter should only translate those into runtime-native form.


So the simplest clean answer is:


Yes, you need a different concept.


Your current “agent plugins” should remain the deployable Claude/Codex/CLI packs.


For NanoClaw, create a separate agent-runtime adapter concept.


And probably also a steward pack concept.


That gives you a stable three-layer model:


agent capability pack   = what an agent can do directly
steward pack            = who the persistent agent is in RAWR
agent runtime adapter   = how RAWR plugs into NanoClaw/OpenClaw


That is concrete, composable, and won’t collapse under its own semantics.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Wait why would we make the entire nanoclaw adapter a plugin? Wouldn’t we just do that directly on the agent host?


I was talking about nanoclaw agent plugins, whatever that means. Is that anything special or is it basically just registering RAWR plugins for use with nanoclaw?


Please actually investigate the nanoclaw docs for this.


Don’t just respond by telling me what I said
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 12s


I checked NanoClaw more closely, and the answer is: no, NanoClaw does not appear to have a real first-class plugin API in the way you’re thinking.


Its philosophy is almost the opposite.


NanoClaw presents itself as a very small, one-process assistant runtime with built-in WhatsApp, persistent SQLite memory, per-group CLAUDE.md memory files, container-isolated agents, and scheduled tasks. It explicitly says “one process, a few source files, no microservices,” and its own site says new capabilities are added by editing the codebase or by using Claude Code skills. NanoClaws.io


The most important line for your question is this one from the NanoClaw site’s testimonials/positioning: “No SDK to learn, no build step, no plugin API. Just a function in src/tools/.” That is not just marketing fluff — it tells you the core extension model is code-level customization of your fork, not a stable runtime plugin contract. NanoClaws.io


Likewise, NanoClaw says additional channels like Telegram, Discord, and Slack are added “via Claude Code skills,” and the site describes those skills as modifying your fork’s source to add dedicated channel support. So the extension story is basically:


edit the codebase,

add tools,

add skills,

restart. NanoClaws.io


That means your earlier framing was off in one important way:


There probably is no special NanoClaw “agent plugin” concept that you should map RAWR onto.


At least from the public docs I found, NanoClaw has:


a runtime,

tools,

channels,

memory/session state,

scheduled tasks,

skills that help Claude work with or modify the system,
but not a clean plugin contract equivalent to “register a runtime plugin with structured metadata and hooks.” NanoClaws.io


So your instinct was closer to correct than my earlier abstraction-heavy answer.


You probably would not make the whole NanoClaw adapter a plugin.


You would most likely implement NanoClaw integration directly in the RAWR agent host.


That is the cleaner model.


Why?


Because NanoClaw is not giving you a rich host-plugin substrate to target. It is giving you a compact agent runtime that you can embed, fork, configure, and extend by code and skills. NanoClaws.io


So the right architecture is:


RAWR host bundle
  -> agent host
      -> NanoClaw runtime integration (direct host-level adapter)
      -> RAWR-exposed tools / channels / memories / steward bindings


Not:


RAWR host bundle
  -> generic plugin loader
      -> NanoClaw plugin


That second version only makes sense if NanoClaw itself had a strong plugin lifecycle model. Based on the docs I found, it does not. NanoClaws.io


So what would “NanoClaw agent plugins” mean in practice?


Probably one of only three things:


First, RAWR tools made available to NanoClaw.
This is the most concrete and likely interpretation. NanoClaw supports adding custom tools as code, and Claude running inside it can use those tools. So a RAWR-to-NanoClaw integration would mainly mean: expose selected RAWR workbench/service operations as NanoClaw tools. NanoClaws.io


Second, skills/instruction packs for Claude inside NanoClaw.
NanoClaw leans on Claude Code skills for extension, especially for adding channels. So your existing deployable Claude/Codex-facing agent packs may actually map better here than I first suggested — not as NanoClaw “plugins,” but as skills/instructions/resources that NanoClaw’s Claude runtime can use. NanoClaws.io


Third, steward/workspace overlays.
NanoClaw already has per-group CLAUDE.md memory files and SQLite-backed persistent state. That means a RAWR steward could have a NanoClaw-side workspace/memory overlay. But this is runtime-local memory, not the structural source of truth for RAWR. NanoClaws.io


So the better question is not “what is a NanoClaw plugin?”
The better question is:


What RAWR artifacts should the agent host project into NanoClaw?


That answer is much cleaner.


I think there are four concrete projections:


RAWR workbench tools
Expose curated RAWR operations to NanoClaw as tools.


Examples:


orient.enter

assess.impact

contribute.scaffold

observe.health

selected internal/private service operations


These should be thin, stable tool wrappers over RAWR APIs/services, not arbitrary raw router dumps.


RAWR agent skill packs
Your existing plugins/agents/* concept — deployable Claude/Codex skill packs — can stay. Those likely become usable by NanoClaw too, because NanoClaw already leans on Claude Code skills as an extension path. So these are not NanoClaw-native plugins; they are cross-runtime agent capability packs. NanoClaws.io

Steward identity + memory bindings
Map RAWR steward identities into NanoClaw session/group/container context:
steward name


role/domain

allowed tools

channel bindings

memory policies

CLAUDE.md overlays
Channel and runtime wiring
If you want WhatsApp/Slack/Telegram ingress, that belongs at the NanoClaw runtime integration layer in the agent host, not in RAWR “agent plugins” per se. NanoClaw already centers channels and scheduling as runtime concerns. NanoClaws.io


So the concrete recommendation is:


Keep your original plugins/agents/* meaning.


That is:
deployable Claude/Codex/agent skill packs.


Do not redefine those as “NanoClaw plugins.”


Instead, implement NanoClaw integration directly in the agent host, and have that host consume:


RAWR tool registrations

RAWR skill packs

RAWR steward definitions

RAWR channel/memory config


That means the actual split should look more like:


plugins/agents/*        # cross-runtime agent skill/capability packs
stewards/*              # steward identity/domain definitions
apps/agent-host/*       # direct NanoClaw integration layer


And then inside the agent host:


NanoClaw runtime
  <- registered RAWR tools
  <- loaded RAWR agent skills/resources
  <- steward-specific memory/context
  <- channel bindings


So to answer your direct question:


No, there does not seem to be anything especially formal or unique called a “NanoClaw plugin” that RAWR should mirror. Based on the docs, NanoClaw’s extension model is mostly:


add code,

add tools,

add skills,

modify your fork,

restart. NanoClaws.io


Therefore, the RAWR move is not to invent a fake NanoClaw-plugin abstraction unless you personally want one for your own host. The better move is:


integrate NanoClaw directly at the agent host layer

treat RAWR agent packs as cross-runtime skill packs

expose RAWR workbench/services as NanoClaw tools

keep steward definitions as a separate RAWR concept


That is both simpler and more faithful to how NanoClaw actually works.


The main uncertainty is that NanoClaw’s public site is the clearest source I found quickly, and it emphasizes simplicity over deep formal docs. I did not find a public, structured extension-spec document that would justify claiming a richer built-in plugin model than what the site says.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: Okay, and what would a steward concretely look like in this system? Right, like, let's say, like, what is the authoring model or the center of gravity within something like nanoclaw? Could we actually define a standalone steward in this way, where basically, like, let's say that one of our, let's say that our nanoclaw plugin would basically just be a declarative config that pulls in any other plugins or routers or clients or whatever from the rawr system, R-A-W-R, and turns them into tools for nanoclaw stewards, and then also allows us to define the steward identity and memory bindings and then some channel or memory config. So would it make sense essentially to kind of like keep things like... The agent skill packs standalone, and then I guess we would also have channels would be plugins, right? Since Nanoclaw expects you to create channels on your own, then I guess we could adopt that as a plugin type into our system and then just hook that up to Nanoclaw. So Nanoclaw gets retrofitted to consume our channel plugin interface, and then it would also consume our agent skills and then steward identity. So basically, like, would it make sense to basically say that for Nanoclaw, the only plugin that we would actually have is a standalone like steward or agent, and then that is basically just a declarative interface that pulls in skill packs plus identity and memory bindings plus, you know, channels. So it kind of just becomes a way to declare an agent. Does that make sense, or is there something I'm missing here?
nodes.source_title: Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Thought for 43s


Yes, that mostly makes sense.


The piece you were missing is the center of gravity.


A steward should not be “whatever NanoClaw needs.”
A steward should be a RAWR-native declaration of persistent agency that NanoClaw consumes.


That matches your product docs. RAWR says identity is stewards: persistent agent identities with domain scope, GitHub accounts, and accumulated decision history, not stateless personas. It also says NanoClaw is the agent dispatch runtime, while RAWR owns the opinions about how the pieces compose.


rawr-product-synthesis


rawr-product-synthesis


So the right model is:


RAWR defines the steward.
NanoClaw runs the steward.


That is the clean split.


The steward authoring model should therefore be declarative and RAWR-first. NanoClaw is just one runtime backend that consumes that declaration. That part of your instinct is right.


Where I would push back is on collapsing everything into “the only plugin is a steward.” That is too much responsibility in one noun. It will become mush fast.


A steward should declare:


identity

domain scope

memory bindings

allowed toolsets / skill packs

channel bindings

runtime preferences


But the steward should reference channels and skill packs, not own their implementation.


Why? Because channels and skill packs are reusable infrastructure or capability surfaces, while a steward is a persistent identity and operating boundary. Your own docs separate persistent steward identity from the workbench operations the steward uses. The workbench is a composed oRPC service with operations like orient, assess, contribute, govern, and observe, and the principle is that if an agent has to grep, infer, cross-reference, or guess, the workbench failed.


steward-workbench-design


steward-workbench-design


So a steward is not primarily “a bag of tools.”
A steward is “an identity plus scope plus memory plus allowed operating surfaces.”


Concretely, I think the right shape is this:


plugins/agents/* stays what you originally meant: cross-runtime skill packs for Claude/Codex/CLI agents.


plugins/channels/* becomes a real thing if you want channel adapters to be first-class in RAWR. That actually fits NanoClaw well, because NanoClaw has WhatsApp built in and expects additional channels like Telegram, Discord, and Slack to be added as code/skills in the fork rather than through a formal plugin API. In other words, NanoClaw does not really give you a plugin system; it gives you a small host that you extend by code, tools, and skills. NanoClaws.io


Then stewards/* becomes the declaration layer.


That gives you three distinct authoring centers:


skill packs: reusable agent capability/instruction/tool overlays

channel plugins: ingress/egress adapters

steward definitions: persistent identities that bind scope, memory, channels, and skill packs together


That is the clean architecture.


So yes, it makes sense for the NanoClaw integration on the RAWR side to mostly consume:


RAWR skill packs

RAWR channel plugins

RAWR steward definitions

RAWR workbench tools


But no, I would not call the steward itself “the NanoClaw plugin.” The steward is higher-level than that.


The simplest concrete mental model is:


RAWR
  defines:
    skill packs
    channel adapters
    steward declarations
    workbench/service tools

agent host
  adapts:
    RAWR declarations -> NanoClaw runtime

NanoClaw
  runs:
    sessions
    channels
    memory
    containers
    long-lived agent execution


That is the right gravity.


What would a steward look like concretely?


Something like this:


export const coordinationSteward = defineSteward({
  id: "coordination-steward",
  displayName: "Coordination Steward",
  domain: {
    scopes: ["@rawr/coordination", "@rawr/coordination-*"],
    agentsMdRoots: ["packages/coordination/AGENTS.md"],
  },
  identity: {
    githubAccount: "rawr-coordination-bot",
    autonomyProfile: "earned",
  },
  memory: {
    kind: "persistent",
    changelog: true,
    decisionHistory: true,
    claudeMdOverlay: "stewards/coordination/CLAUDE.md",
  },
  channels: [
    { ref: "local-dev-console" },
    { ref: "slack-engineering" },
  ],
  skills: [
    { ref: "rawr-core-navigation" },
    { ref: "rawr-contract-authoring" },
    { ref: "coordination-domain-pack" },
  ],
  tools: [
    { ref: "workbench.orient" },
    { ref: "workbench.assess" },
    { ref: "workbench.contribute" },
    { ref: "workbench.govern" },
    { ref: "workbench.observe" },
  ],
  runtime: {
    backend: "nanoclaw",
    containerProfile: "repo-write",
    swarmRole: "lead",
  },
});


That is a steward.


Notice what it is not doing:


it is not implementing Slack

it is not implementing the skill pack

it is not implementing the tool

it is not implementing NanoClaw itself


It is just binding those things into a persistent agent identity.


That is exactly the level you want.


Now the NanoClaw side.


NanoClaw’s actual center of gravity is:


one small process

channels

SQLite-backed persistent state

per-group CLAUDE.md memory

isolated containers

scheduled tasks

tools added directly in code

channels beyond WhatsApp added via code/skills in your fork, not through a real formal plugin API NanoClaws.io


That means the RAWR-to-NanoClaw adapter on the agent host should do translation, not invention.


It should:


register RAWR tools as NanoClaw tools

map RAWR channel plugins into NanoClaw channel handlers

materialize steward-specific memory overlays into NanoClaw’s storage / CLAUDE.md conventions

load steward skill packs into the runtime context

start one or more NanoClaw identities/sessions according to steward declarations


So the authoring center remains RAWR declarations, while NanoClaw is the runtime executor.


That also means your statement “our NanoClaw plugin would basically just be a declarative config” is close, but I would rename it.


It is not really a “NanoClaw plugin.”
It is better thought of as a steward declaration consumed by the agent host’s NanoClaw adapter.


That sounds pedantic, but it matters because NanoClaw itself does not present a serious first-class plugin API. Its own site leans hard on “small enough to understand,” “one process,” “no microservices,” and “no plugin API; just a function in src/tools/.” NanoClaws.io


So the architecture should reflect reality:


NanoClaw is not the plugin host

RAWR is


That is the core correction.


Now, on channels specifically: yes, I think channels should be a separate plugin type if you want this to be portable and pluggable in RAWR. That gives you a stable abstraction independent of NanoClaw, and then the NanoClaw adapter can consume that interface and wire it into NanoClaw’s runtime model.
