nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
You do not need the folders today if an app assembly is just:


one rawr.hq.ts

one thin entrypoint per role

maybe one config file


But you probably still should keep the folders.


Because this is one of those cases where the folder is buying you topology stability, not current implementation necessity.


That matters for:


agent navigation

scaffolding consistency

future growth without churn

clear promotion path

keeping “assembly” as a first-class thing


So the honest answer is:


Functionally: no, not always.
Architecturally/topologically: yes, probably.


The key distinction is between a thing being a real project node versus merely a file.


In your system, apps/hq, apps/sales, apps/todo are not just “files that happen to start something.” They are potential assembly roots. Even when they are tiny, they represent:


a bounded host bundle

a composition authority

a deployment unit candidate

a promotion boundary

an ownership/topology node


That is enough reason to give them a stable home.


If you reduce them to only single files floating around, you save almost nothing, but you lose a lot of clarity. Agents and humans both benefit from being able to treat “sales app assembly” as a place, not just a filename.


So I would keep the structure like:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Even if those directories are thin.


That said, I would not overbuild them either.


The right rule is:


An app assembly folder should start life as a thin stable container, not a mini-codebase.


So yes, the folder exists.
But most real logic should still live in:


services/*

plugins/*

packages/*

packages/hq-sdk/*


The app folder should mostly contain:


composition file(s)

role entrypoints

local app config

maybe deployment descriptors later


That is all.


This is one of those “don’t shake the base” decisions. You want the scaffold shape to be stable even if the implementation inside it is initially small.


There is also an Nx reason to keep the folder.


Nx thinks in terms of projects. A project that represents an assembly root is easier to reason about, tag, generate, own, and promote if it has a stable directory. That gives you:


cleaner project identity

clearer graph nodes

less churn when the assembly grows

better generator targets

better topology for agents


So if the question is:


“Should apps/sales be a folder even if it only contains one or two files for now?”


My answer is yes.


If the question is:


“Should every role under every app always get a folder from day one?”


That is less absolute.


You could compress early if you want:


apps/
  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts
    async.ts


and later expand to:


apps/
  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts


That is a reasonable compromise.


But I would still keep the app root folder itself.


So the hierarchy I’d recommend is:


Keep apps/<assembly>/ as a stable directory node.
Inside it, allow role entrypoints to start either as files or folders depending on how much structure you already need.


That gives you both:


stable topology

low initial overhead


So the best near-term shape is probably:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts
    async.ts
    cli.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server.ts


Then when one role grows, expand just that role:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
      config.ts
    async/
      main.ts
      workers.ts


That is the most leverage-efficient version.


So the final answer is:


keep the apps/<assembly> folders

do not feel forced to make every role a folder immediately

treat app folders as stable topology nodes for humans, agents, Nx, and future promotion

keep them thin so the real logic stays elsewhere


That is the cleanest balance.

nodes.say: yeah but that leaves variability for little gain right
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Yes. That variability is probably not worth it.


If your goal is stable topology for humans, agents, scaffolding, and Nx project identity, then pick one shape and keep it uniform.


So I would standardize on:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts
    async/
      main.ts
    cli/
      main.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts
    server/
      main.ts


Even when the role folder is thin.


Why? Because the gain is not in today’s file count. The gain is in removing conditional structure.


If some assemblies use:


server.ts


and others use:


server/main.ts


then every generator, graph assumption, agent heuristic, and human expectation now has to handle both.


That is noise.


The architecture you are building benefits more from uniformity than from shaving one folder level.


This is one of those places where the right move is boring:
choose the slightly more explicit structure and make it universal.


The cost is tiny:


+ one extra folder
+ one extra main.ts


The benefit is real:


zero ambiguity

easier generation

easier promotion

easier agent traversal

easier role-local growth

cleaner Nx project conventions

fewer special cases forever


So yes, your pushback is right. Allowing both shapes is unnecessary variability.


I would lock:


apps/<assembly>/rawr.hq.ts

apps/<assembly>/<role>/main.ts


for every assembly/role that exists.


No mixed shorthand.
No “flatten until needed.”
No alternate micro-shapes.


Just one shape.


That is the better base.
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say: but wait… what even goes into those folderV
nodes.source_title: Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Branch · Server Sidecar Process Model

nodes.say:
Very little, by design.


Those folders should be assembly entrypoint folders, not where the real app logic lives.


So apps/<assembly>/<role>/ exists mostly to hold the runtime-specific glue for that assembled host role.


The rule should be:


If something is reusable across assemblies, it does not belong there.
If something is domain/business logic, it does not belong there.
If something is a runtime-entry concern for this exact assembly+role, it probably does.


So in:


apps/hq/server/
apps/hq/async/
apps/sales/server/


the contents should be things like:


main.ts
The actual entrypoint. Boot the role runtime, load the assembly, start the process.


config.ts
Only if this assembly+role has local config shaping that is not generic enough for the host SDK.


env.ts
Only if you want explicit environment parsing/binding local to that role entrypoint.


telemetry.ts
Only if there is role-local telemetry bootstrap that should happen before startup.


routes.ts or mount.ts
Only if you need a tiny local composition wrapper before handing off to the SDK bootstrap.


README.md
Optional, but useful if the role has non-obvious startup/deploy semantics.


That’s basically it.


What should not go there:


service logic

plugin logic

reusable host bootstrap logic

shared schemas

domain workflows

business rules

general utilities


Those belong in:


services/*

plugins/*

packages/*

packages/hq-sdk/*


So the ideal main.ts is almost boring.


Example for server:


import { startServerRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/server";
import { createHqAssembly } from "../rawr.hq";

const assembly = createHqAssembly();

await startServerRole({
  assembly,
});


Example for async:


import { startAsyncRole } from "@rawr/hq-sdk/roles/async";
import { createHqAssembly } from "../rawr.hq";

const assembly = createHqAssembly();

await startAsyncRole({
  assembly,
});


That is the point. The folder is not there because it is full of code. It is there because it is a stable topology node.


So think of the contents as falling into three buckets.


First, entrypoint glue.
The minimal code needed to start that exact role for that exact assembly.


Second, role-local config/bootstrap.
Env parsing, startup flags, transport bindings, telemetry setup, shutdown hooks.


Third, deployment-adjacent metadata.
Anything specific to how this role is run, built, or deployed.


If a folder has more than that, something is probably leaking into the wrong layer.


A concrete shape could be:


apps/
  hq/
    rawr.hq.ts

    server/
      main.ts
      env.ts
      config.ts

    async/
      main.ts
      env.ts

  sales/
    rawr.hq.ts

    server/
      main.ts


And many of those files may stay tiny for a long time.


Sanity check:


Question: could this file be shared across assemblies?
Yes -> put it in hq-sdk/package/service/plugin layer.

Question: is this file about starting this exact assembly role?
Yes -> it can live in apps/<assembly>/<role>/.


So the blunt answer is:


Almost nothing goes in those folders.
That’s why they’re good.
They are stable runtime entrypoint containers, not logic buckets.
